planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown transplants in late winter to early spring in deep, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep-soiled sites in full sun; pecan develops a taproot up to 5 feet deep within its first few years and requires deep, loose, non-compacted soil for best growth and production — avoid shallow, rocky, or compacted sites.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; pecans require at least 30–40 feet of space in all directions and are best suited to large properties with deep, loamy soil. Carya illinoinensis (pecan) is the iconic native nut tree of the American South and Southwest — the only major North American nut to have achieved worldwide commercial importance — producing sweet, buttery, thin-shelled nuts from September through November that are consumed fresh, candied, in pies (pecan pie is the most iconic Southern dessert), and in a vast range of confectioneries and baked goods. The pecan is native to the Mississippi-Ohio River basin and Gulf Coastal Plain, reaching 60–100 feet tall and living for centuries; established trees are drought-resistant and incredibly long-lived. Two trees with different bloom times (Type I — shedding pollen before receptive to it; Type II — the opposite) are needed for reliable cross-pollination and maximum nut production. Named varieties for the Gulf South include 'Desirable,' 'Candy,' 'Caddo,' 'Cheyenne,' 'Kiowa,' and 'Oconee' — all selected for disease resistance to pecan scab, the primary disease problem in humid southeastern climates. The trees also have significant wildlife value as a mast crop for deer, turkeys, and squirrels.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pecan Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Varieties
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pecans

planting.note: Plant container stock or bare-root in spring in well-drained, rocky, caliche, or sandy, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Texas persimmon is native to rocky limestone soils of central and south Texas and northern Mexico and is extremely drought-tolerant and heat-adapted.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky to sandy, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; once established, Texas black persimmon requires little to no supplemental water. Diospyros texana (Texas black persimmon or Mexican persimmon) is a native small tree or large shrub of rocky Texas Hill Country limestone and the brushlands of south Texas and northern Mexico, producing small, 1-inch, round, jet-black fruits in July–September that taste intensely sweet — like dark plums or figs with a honey-like richness — and stain everything they touch with deep-purple-black juice (historically used as a fabric dye by Native Americans and early settlers). The tree is highly ornamental in addition to its fruit value: an attractive, multi-stemmed form with flaky, pale-gray bark that exfoliates to reveal silvery, cream, and pale-orange patches; small, glossy, semi-evergreen leaves; and outstanding fall-winter structural interest. It is adapted to full sun and extreme drought and alkaline soils where most fruit trees struggle. Wildlife (deer, wild turkey, quail, and many bird species) consume the fruits eagerly. Texas persimmon is dioecious (separate male and female trees needed for fruit); plant at least one male per 3–5 female trees.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Fruit Plants

planting.note: Plant bare-root stock or container plants in early spring before bud break in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Chickasaw plum is one of the toughest native fruits for the Southeast and Gulf South, tolerating sandy soils and drought that defeat most fruit plants.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Chickasaw plum thrives on neglect and is essentially care-free once established. Prunus angustifolia (Chickasaw plum or sand plum) is one of the most important native fruit plants of the Gulf Coastal Plain, East Texas, and Piedmont Southeast — a tough, fast-growing, suckering shrub or small tree producing abundant 1/2-to-3/4-inch, bright-red to orange-red or yellow plums in May–June that are tart-sweet and excellent for jellies, preserves, wines, and eating out of hand. The tree is one of the earliest flowering native plants of the Southeast, producing dense clusters of small white flowers in February–March (before the leaves emerge) that provide extremely valuable early nectar for native bees emerging from winter dormancy. Chickasaw plum forms thickets by root suckers, creating dense, thorny hedgerows that provide outstanding nesting cover and food for a wide range of wildlife. It is self-fertile (though cross-pollination improves yields) and one of the most heat, drought, and disease-resistant of all stone fruits for southeastern gardens.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Fruits
North Carolina State Extension — Native Fruit Plants

common_name: Darrow Blueberry (Evergreen Blueberry, Native Florida Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Vaccinium darrowii is native to scrub, flatwoods, and dry pinelands of Florida and southeastern Georgia and is adapted to the sandy, acidic, well-drained soils of the coastal plain — it requires more acidic soil than commercial blueberry types.

directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich, sandy to moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun; amend with peat moss or pine bark to increase acidity; mulch heavily with pine straw. Vaccinium darrowii (Darrow blueberry or evergreen blueberry) is a native Florida and southeast Georgia blueberry species — one of several native blueberries of the southeastern coastal plain — producing small, 1/4-inch, intensely flavored, blue-black berries in April–May with an extraordinarily rich, sweet-tart blueberry flavor that many consider superior to commercial cultivars. The small, semi-evergreen to evergreen, blue-green leaves are among the smallest of any blueberry species; the white, urn-shaped flowers in February–March are especially important for native bee pollinators emerging early. This species is a parent of several southern highbush blueberry cultivars (including 'Sharpblue,' 'Gulf Coast,' and 'O'Neal') bred for low-chill southern climates. Darrow blueberry is extremely low-maintenance in native soils — requiring no supplemental irrigation once established and producing reliably in the conditions of the coastal plain with minimal care. It is rarely available commercially but is outstanding as a native wildlife plant — birds and small mammals consume the berries eagerly.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Blueberries

common_name: Mexican Plum (Big Tree Plum, Creek Plum)

planting.note: Plant bare-root stock in early spring in well-drained, limestone, caliche, or loamy, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Mexican plum is native to limestone cedar glades, stream banks, and forest margins in central Texas and Oklahoma and is exceptionally adapted to alkaline, calcareous soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral or slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Mexican plum tolerates rocky, shallow, limestone soils that defeat most fruit trees. Prunus mexicana (Mexican plum or big tree plum) is the largest native plum of Texas and a landscape-worthy ornamental tree as well as a valuable food plant — more tree-like in form than the suckering Chickasaw plum, growing to 15–25 feet as a single-trunked specimen with attractive, exfoliating, reddish-brown bark. The 1-to-1.5-inch, dark-red to purple-red plums ripen in August–September with a rich, sweet-tart flavor excellent for jams, jellies, preserves, and fresh eating. Clouds of fragrant white flowers smother the bare branches in February–March, making it one of the most spectacular early-flowering native trees in the Texas landscape. Mexican plum is self-fertile, requiring no cross-pollination; it is also one of the most limestone and drought-tolerant of the native plums, thriving in the alkaline, rocky soils of the Texas Hill Country, Edwards Plateau, and Post Oak Savanna. Wildlife value is high — the large fruits are consumed by deer, foxes, raccoons, and many bird species.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Fruit Plants
